perturbation.original_text: In the event that a party materially breaches any representation or covenant herein, the other party may give written notice of the breach requiring the same to be remedied within 30 days of receipt of such notice. If the breaching party fails to remedy the material breach in such time period, the non-breaching party may terminate this Agreement by delivery of a written termination notice to the breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a party materially breaches any representation or covenant herein, the other party may give written notice of the breach requiring the same to be remedied within 10 days of receipt of such notice. If the breaching party fails to remedy the material breach in such time period, the non-breaching party may terminate this Agreement by delivery of a written termination notice to the breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to 10 days may be considered unreasonably short under New York law, potentially conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-309(3) states that termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. A 10-day period to cure a material breach might be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity and nature of the breach, thus conflicting with the UCC's requirement for reasonable notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-308 Absence of Specified Place for Delivery up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-310 Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-309 Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation says this timeframe might be unreasonable and conflict with UCC requirements for reasonable notification. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NY UCC § 2-309(3), states that termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification' and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Because the reduced cure period might be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity and nature of the breach, this creates a contradiction. The modified text potentially conflicts with the UCC's requirement for reasonable notification, making the contract term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to its principles of conflicts of laws, other than Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to its principles of conflicts of laws, other than Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware while still referencing a specific provision (Section 5-1401) of the New York General Obligations Law creates a legal inconsistency and ambiguity. Section 5-1401 is exclusive to New York law and will not be applicable in Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: By specifying Delaware law, it is implied that Delaware law should govern the agreement. Delaware has its own legal code and procedures, and specifying a New York statute inherently creates confusion about what law is to be actually followed. While not strictly illegal, this ambiguity undermines the intent and enforceability of the governing law provision.

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law as the governing law while simultaneously referencing a specific provision (Section 5-1401) of the New York General Obligations Law. This creates a legal inconsistency because Section 5-1401 is exclusive to New York law and not applicable under Delaware law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this ambiguity, indicating that the inclusion of a New York statute under Delaware law undermines the enforceability of the governing law provision. Scraped Law Snippet 1 relates to Delaware contract law, further solidifying that Delaware law would govern the agreement, thus excluding the applicability of New York law and creating an inconsistency. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction because the contract contains obligations that are in conflict with each other.
